speaker: interviewer
text: An interview with Mr. Stefan Stok. So at the end of seventh tape, you were talking about celebrating your ninth birthday in a shelter down--

speaker: interviewee
text: In the cellar.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the cellar, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you were talking about action that were going on in the street.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, and there were-- we were actually were already then that the war is going on, because the Soviets were occasionally bombarding, bombing, Warsaw. And one occasion that I remember, that must have been end of August or early September, that my father, and I, and an aunt, we were all locked in the office in the factory on the third floor, and locked by my uncle, because that was supposed to be a safer place in case the Germans would come in the night. And the horrible bombardment started by the Soviets, by the Russians. So we were on the third floor of the factory, in the large industrial complex, which was not the best place to be during the bombardment. And it was very-- and we couldn't get out, because the door was locked. The window was covered with a iron grill.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was a complete trap. But even then, one was somehow had a feeling of certain elation when one knew that the Soviets are bombing, that there is an air bombardment by the Soviets, because one felt that there is still somebody who is actually fighting the Germans, which for us it almost seemed as if the rest of the world didn't exist anymore, that everything was-- it was if the war was-- we didn't feel that the war was still going on.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the action was going on in this way until the 6th of September. And on the 6th of September--

speaker: interviewee
text: '42, it was all '42. It was all a business of six weeks, approximately, since the later part of July. And in September, suddenly, things were happening all the time suddenly, without any-- obviously, it's a surprise, horrible surprise. And, suddenly, there was an announcement. I don't know exactly how it happened. Suddenly, it was announced that the whole population of ghetto, whoever remained, was going to assemble in a very small area near [POLISH] Street, and in a number of blocks of houses around in [POLISH] Street, in the north of the ghetto, not far from the Umschlagplatz. And that there is going to be another selection. And everybody had to be there with their small amount of belongings, and so on, at 10:00 o'clock in the morning. At 10:00 o'clock, the [POLISH] area, all of this, and the rest of the ghetto, all had to be empty of people. And I remember again, this morning was-- again, everybody was panic-stricken. And there were still many, many people pushing their little carts, and running this way and that way, like frightened animals, not knowing which way to go, and which way to-- but we-- that means my parents, my uncles, and so on-- nobody wanted to go to this [POLISH] Street, because nobody saw anything that there was-- everybody saw that it might be impossible to get rescued from there. And we decided to stay behind. And we tried. And it was not easy to stay behind, because the place where we-- the cellar where we hid in on my birthday, somebody else was there already. And we tried to get into some bunker. And it was completely full. So there was no way. And we finally, all of us, that means my uncles, and aunts, and my parents, and myself, about all together-- not all of them, but about 10 people, or maybe 12 people-- we all hid behind some kind of a very basic partition. We were kind of a pretty basic hiding place in the flat where we lived then in this-- in the [POLISH] flat opposite the factory, and all squashed into this very narrow space, maybe something like two meter one way and half a meter the other way, covered with plywood, and practically with no possibility to breathe even. I was almost suffocating there, because I was sitting on the side, and somehow managing to breath, but hardly. And we spent-- and then 10:00 o'clock passed. And then Germans promised that they are going to search everything, search the flats. And if anybody is going to be found out, they are going to be shot on the spot. And they would do it, of course. So we heard them coming. And we heard them breaking into the flats on the first floor, and then breaking into the flats on the second floor. And if they would come into our flat, a very good likelihood that they would find us, because it was too primitive as a hiding place. But, finally, they went into the second floor. But they didn't come to the floor-- to our floor. It was already enough for them of work for this morning. And they went somewhere else.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were saved once again. And we stayed there still quite some time, until in the evening we crawled out. And we cooked some potatoes, something to eat. And we stayed the night in the flat. But hardly anybody slept.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we didn't know what was going to happen. We didn't know if people were-- if the Jews were going to come back. Or maybe they would take them all from [POLISH] Street, and take them God knows where. Nobody knew if they were coming, or if they were not coming, and what was going to happen. But next morning, after we were waiting, and crawling out of the-- or looking out of the window, and out of the balconies, crawling into the balcony to see if they-- because there was nobody in the street. It was all empty. And then we saw, suddenly, groups of people coming back. They came back at maybe 11:00 o'clock, next day, after 24 hours. And what happened was they came back. And we were saved again once. And what happened was that they really concentrated all the remaining population, legal population, of the ghetto in [POLISH] Street and in the nearby streets. And there was a kind of cattle kind of encirclement. And they then made a selection place, where they were marching everybody out of there, and marching them either left to the Umschlagplatz, or right back to the ghetto. And, of course, that went according to their pure ideas of the SS, whom they-- and the children had no chance anyway. So the children that survived, they were usually carried in the rucksacks of their parents, or rucksacks, or sacks, or packages, whatever they were carrying, if there were any that-- and, great, so some people, those who were young, and strong, and looked more or less still presentable, were sent back to life. And the rest were sent to Umschlagplatz.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that happened. And then later, Germans for days, and days, and days, were clearing those blocks of houses, and getting people who were hiding to Umschlagplatz. And some people still saved themselves from [POLISH] Street, although it was not easy.

speaker: interviewee
text: And after that happened, practically the great action was in Warsaw was finished for the time being. And out of maybe 350 or 400,000 people before the action in the ghetto, at least 300,000 went to Treblinka. But some were left. There were still maybe, I don't know how many, maybe 70,000, maybe 60,000, maybe 70,000 people left in the ghetto. Some of them were working officially in the shops. There was still some Jewish police. There were still some-- there was still probably Judenrat to an extent. And there were probably still tens of thousands official ones, and more tens of thousands who were unofficial, who were the wild ones, as we called them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wild ones?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wild ones, because they were unofficial. They were illegals, the ones who didn't have any papers, who were just hiding in the wild houses.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wild houses?

speaker: interviewee
text: Houses which were abandoned, which were already abandoned, because now ghetto was-- there were not enough people to live in this big area. So ghetto was now parcelled, divided, sectioned. The small ghetto, so-called, was put to become part of Polish area of Warsaw. That became just part of Poles moved in there. And in the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Poles, not Jewish people moved in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, how could-- there were no Jewish people left. I mean, Poles, probably. The wall was partly destroyed. And there was that part of ghetto, previous part of ghetto, was now incorporated in the Polish area. And Poles were now receiving permission to move into the ex-Jewish flats, and to inherit the ex-Jewish flats, and houses, and possessions. And some were very pleased, because after all, there was plenty to inherit. And the other part of the ghetto, the big ghetto, was as well not wholly inhabited, because there were-- it was big. The population, most of it has gone. So there were now what was called terrains, areas, belonged to shuls, belonged to [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was this area of maybe two streets surrounded by barbed wire, with their own factory guard, police, [POLISH]. [POLISH], that means that was guard of the factory. Not anymore Jewish police, necessarily, maybe they were Jewish as well, but they were only belonging to the factory. And you couldn't go in. And you couldn't go out without the permission.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you lived there. You lived on this terrain. And everything-- so there were maybe three or four, I don't know how many factory areas like that, where the majority of people who survived until then were living. And the remainder of the area was empty, just big area of empty houses, with empty rooms, empty flats, open flats where everything was still there. The sinks were there. The furniture was there. Only the people weren't there. And some people-- those were the wild houses. That was the wild area. And some people were living still there, wild ones. And they survived still maybe for a time, until they were caught, or until-- because, after all, Germans were doing occasionally still searches, and so on. But I think the main commander left Warsaw, or at least was not active in Warsaw. And for the rest of this autumn and winter, there were no actions in the same way as before. There were a few actions. There was an action on Yom Kippur. I think Germans were very keen on Jewish holidays for those purposes. That was Yom Kippur and the great-- and when the uprising went next year in '43 that started, that was on Passover. That was, as well, Hitler's birthday. But that's something else. And so they knew very well. They were quite informed about Jewish holidays. So they did on Yom Kippur, an action. And then there was a bit of peace and quiet. I mean, not that anybody had anymore illusions, not that people thought that everything was now finished, and those that remained would remain. But for the time being, it didn't seem so terrible. There were no actions. But the Jews, as I said, were without illusions. And I think by then, everybody knew that those people who were resettled, who were taken out, who were moved, that they were not, in fact, resettled, but they were all dead. I think everybody realized by then. Rumors were there before, rumors during the action when they were talking about what was-- the common thing that were said were that they were making custom soap. That was a well known expression, that they make them to soap. You know, that people are going to be made into soap, that the fat content is going to be-- and, indeed, there were some experiments with soap.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that wasn't-- you know that in Israel-- you know what [NON-ENGLISH] means? And so on, so that expression, soap, I already remember it from '42. But one didn't know, I think, in '42 still during the action all the details. Some people did, because some people came even back from Treblinka, but few.

speaker: interviewee
text: But in '42 after the action, then it slowly, everybody started-- it was found out. It was more and more people knew about more and more details. And there were some people who escaped from Treblinka, and from transports, and so on. So it became obvious. And then people started digging bunkers. Building--

speaker: interviewer
text: In the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the ghetto, in the ghetto. There were bunkers everywhere. They started building bunkers deep into the ground, and putting-- the building them properly, with bricks, and with concrete, and with furniture, putting bunk beds into them, and connecting them to water supply, and to electricity supply, and-- and to canalization, to the drainage system, so that some of those bunkers were really brilliant. Some of the bunkers were well excellently built, and excellently equipped. And many people lived in them. Many, many people lived in those bunkers. But, of course, nobody realized that what was this area of big houses, and big blocks of houses, and huge-- which was still intact at this time, was still there. The houses were there. The people were not there. But the houses, it was intact, all untouched, apart from a few holes where the bombs fell. But otherwise, it was all there. And nobody realized that nothing would remain of that, absolutely nothing, not a house, not a wall.

speaker: interviewer
text: So one thought those bunkers were maybe-- people thought maybe they would survive the war in those bunkers. And there was this strange period of hiatus, of quietness, for months, when some people were terribly active. But other people were not so active.

speaker: interviewee
text: And just-- my father was working in the factory. And we lived then-- we had another flat then on the factory area [POLISH], around the corner, not very far. And we lived there. And we slept there mostly, because, really, the times were a bit quiet. And that was late in winter '42, maybe beginning '43. But the general feeling was, like trauma, aftershock. Everybody was still living in this state of-- nobody expected anything good. There was a brother of my father, the younger brother of my father, who spent-- who went with us to Rowne in the beginning of the war. And he was a Jewish policeman. And he was a very good looking young man, who was the youngest brother of my father. He was big, and fair haired, and a brilliant driver. He was even driving in rallies before the war, and with all kinds of Polish friends, and very lucky with women. And he was absolutely-- for him, it should have been possible to survive the war. And he was, as I said, a Jewish policeman.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the ghetto, also in the ghetto. And I don't know exactly what he did. But what Jewish policemen-- maybe he was not as bad as some others, and maybe no. I don't know exactly. But he had a wife who was a very pretty woman, and a little daughter, who was my cousin, who was the youngest cousin of me, who was born in 1937. And her name was Pucia. And I was very fond of her. I saw her very often before the war, and still-- and then she was five. And she-- at the end of the action in September, when the Jewish police, of course, were guaranteed survival. And then on one occasion, they were employed somewhere in the ghetto to do a blockade or God knows what. And the other group of Jewish police were told to surround the houses where they lived. And they took the families of their friends, of their colleagues, of Jewish police, to Umschlagplatz. And my uncle's wife and child went to Treblinka in September.

speaker: interviewer
text: So other Jewish police took the families of their colleagues?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of their own colleagues, yes, yes. And so they went to Treblinka. And when-- and he was-- he, of course, still survived. And he was broken down then. And he was coming to my father in this flat in [POLISH] late autumn, maybe in winter '42. And he was crying all the time. I mean, he was still a young man. He was maybe 32, 33, and a big, strong bloke. But he really broke down. And what happened to him was that because he was-- he spoke perfect German, because he spent years of his in Berlin and in Dresden. He was went there to a trade school, to a [GERMAN], and spent-- and in Berlin. So he spoke beautiful German. And he was altogether, as I said, a brilliant driver, and a handsome chap. So the Germans still employed him. He was working as a driver for the SS, and still in '43. They had those-- they had the what was called [POLISH]. They had different places for work for Jewish workers in the area inside of Warsaw, which went on after the ghetto already disappeared. And he was still working for them in '43, after the ghetto was already finished, until one day they, very unwillingly, very unhappily, obviously, they decided that they must finish with them as well. They took the group of Jewish workers, and shot them down near the [INAUDIBLE]. That was the end of him. But he could have-- he could have saved himself. He could have really saved himself. He had better, how to say, chances than all of us otherwise. He was stronger, and better looking, and fair-haired, and handsome, and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: But he was-- [POLISH], Simone. But he didn't have strengths, and in particular--

speaker: interviewer
text: After they took his--

speaker: interviewee
text: And after they took his wife and child, he certainly-- he was a broken man. And I remember that they were sitting there, my father and him in the evening, and drinking something, next to that paraffin lamp, or carbide lamp. There was a carbide lamp that was-- gave very strong, white light. And he was crying most of the time. My father was the only person that-- the only family member that remained. Anyway, and carbide lamps were dangerous. Carbide lamps were-- one night, my father-- there was maybe a [INAUDIBLE] or something. And my father lit that carbide lamp. And I was sleeping with him in that room. And after he switched-- and there was suddenly this strong light in the room. And I looked. And I was so shocked.

speaker: interviewer
text: I couldn't even say what was-- my father looked at me. And maybe he asked, what? Where the light came from? And I said to him-- I couldn't get the words out, I was so shocked.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said Papa, Tata in Polish. Papa, your hair is burning. And his hair caught fire from the carbide lamp, and was completely burned, burned altogether. Well, it grew again. But it was-- so carbide lamps were not safe.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in January, there was-- January '43?

speaker: interviewee
text: January '43, yes, and I remember of this period-- well, I remember all kinds of things. I remember Germans coming, and the groups of maybe army. I don't know if it was army or SS, coming, and singing their songs, marching through the ghetto, and singing those songs. And I remember houses burning. They already then were burning groups of houses, of those wild houses. Maybe it was just a fire that started and got out of control. There was this tremendous, tremendous fire. And January came. And in January, there was another action. But in January, that action was in mid-January, maybe 15th of January. And then the Jews didn't appear anymore. They didn't get out. They hid in the bunkers. They didn't come out to be taken. They didn't allow themselves to be taken. And the action was-- and we hid in a bunker as well. We hid in a bunker. In Nowolipki, there was a bunker, which was-- somehow, there was a dentist surgery somewhere. And under the dentist's chair, they made an entrance to the bunker-- or behind the dentist's chair, next to the dentist's chair-- there was an entrance which was well-masked this time. It was, this time, a professional job. And there was a bunker, quite a big bunker with many people there. And we hid there. And that was pretty unsuccessful, that action. And the Germans must have then realize that the Jews were not anymore allowing themselves to be taken in the same way. And, OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is the end of tape eight.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's end of the ghetto--
